text: The surgeon performs a series of intercostal blocks under direct vision, using a green needle to administer anesthesia into each intercostal space from the external view.
The surgeon uses a Maryland wristed instrument and a Johan grasper, both capable of applying bipolar energy, to manipulate tissues during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses the Johan grasper to pull down tissue while employing the Maryland instrument for diathermy to remove fat from the posterior surface of the sternum as CO₂ insufflation begins.
The surgeon removes adipose tissue from the posterior aspect of the sternum, creating additional space for the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the left internal mammary artery, noting its position medial to the usual approach, and observes a brief glimpse of the artery during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a left VATS approach to create space for the procedure, aiming to dissect down to the diaphragm. Instruments are maneuvered to facilitate exposure, considering the anatomical position of the left superior vena cava.
The surgeon observes the left phrenic nerve following the left superior vena cava (SVC), using wristed instruments to achieve an optimal angle for dissection.
The surgeon uses bipolar instruments to carefully dissect along the phrenic nerve, leaving a small margin and exposing the phrenic nerve and the left superior vena cava (SVC).

The surgeon carefully performs blunt dissection near the phrenic nerve using Maryland instruments, ensuring a safe distance before applying bipolar energy for controlled cauterization. The instruments provide precise energy distribution with minimal dissipation.
The surgeon follows the left superior vena cava (SVC) and examines the medial border, searching for a vestigial innominate vein, which is not visible in the operation.
The surgeon carefully follows the phrenic nerve downwards, inspecting the pre-thymic fat for potential n-one lymph nodes, which are significant according to the IASLC eighth edition thymic staging.

The surgeon carefully dissects the fat off the back of the sternum, pulling down the thymus while inspecting for any abnormal drop of the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) vein. The LIMA vein is visible connecting to the left superior vena cava (SVC) as the dissection progresses to the right of the Maryland instrument.

The surgeon identifies the blood supply to the thymus, observing the Lima and Rima veins and arteries entering the thymic tissue. The surgeon carefully examines potential drainage pathways for the thymic vein, including the Lima vein, Rima vein, left SVC, and right SVC.

The surgeon uses a hook ligature instrument, which can divide vessels up to seven millimeters, to manipulate and potentially divide one of the vessels associated with the thymus. The instrument is shown with a hook on the side, allowing for quick transformation from ligature to hook functionality.

The surgeon uses the Hook Ligashore to carefully dissect and divide a vessel within the tissue stalk, suspecting it to be one of the thymic vessels originating from the lemur stumps. The tissue is pulled away to reveal the vessel, ensuring safe division with the Ligashore tool.

The surgeon uses the hook diathermy part of the hook ligature to remove fat down to the diaphragm, ensuring complete fat removal in a myasthenic patient. The hook is used for speed, while the ligature provides better hemostasis. The dissection continues until the diaphragm muscles are visible.

The surgeon uses the hook ligature with the left hand to divide fat from the back of the sternum while pulling tissue with the right hand. The procedure progresses as more fat is removed from the chest wall, preparing to move across the chest to locate the reamer.

The surgeon uses a hook and ligature to manipulate the thymus, pressing down on the pericardium to separate the thymus from the diaphragm in the right hemithorax. CO₂ insufflation is applied to maintain visibility and space within the thoracic cavity.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to push thymic tissue away from the diaphragm, revealing the bottom of the pericardium. The diaphragm is visible to the left, and the thymus is being pulled up with the right hand. The Maryland's bipolar function aids in dividing the thymus from the diaphragm, progressing along the pericardium and exploring natural tissue planes.

The surgeon identifies a tissue plane in the non-tumor area and strips it off the pericardium, aiming to take a substantial portion of the pericardium to ensure complete resection. The right pleura is opened, and the surgeon tracks southward to connect with the right diaphragm, using diathermy to dissect down the back of the sternum and across to the opposite side of the chest. The diaphragm is visible extending off to the side.

The surgeon uses a hook instrument to divide and remove fatty tissue from the right hemidiaphragm, achieving a clear view of the diaphragm with minimal remaining fat. The camera provides a comprehensive view from the left chest to the right diaphragm, showcasing the effectiveness of VATS and wristed instruments in this approach.

The surgeon removes fat from the back of the sternum while managing CO₂ levels due to hypercarbia. The right lung is inflated, and the procedure continues in the left chest, approaching the thymoma. The surgeon assesses proximity to the thymoma before deciding to open the pericardium, aiming to avoid excessive closeness to ensure a clean resection.

The surgeon provides a clear view of the left superior vena cava (SVC) as it extends into the pericardium, where it joins the coronary sinus of the heart. The absence of an innominate vein is noted, and the next visual focus is on the arch of the aorta.

The surgeon dissects around the arch of the aorta, revealing the left subclavian and left carotid arteries, as well as the innominate arteries. The vagus nerve is visible crossing over the arch of the aorta. The dissection progresses towards the neck area.

The surgeon navigates high into the neck to access the left superior sulcus, using CO₂ insufflation to aid visibility. The thyro-thymic ligament and surrounding fat are exposed. A hook ligature is applied, and the surgeon assesses the pericardium, noting its tension. The pericardium is grasped and manipulated to allow safe bipolar cautery, resulting in a controlled entry into the pericardium.

The surgeon uses a bipolar ligature to safely manipulate tissue without risk of burning the heart, while advancing towards the thymoma. A large pericardial patch is created from phrenic to phrenic without the need for reconstruction, ensuring the lungs remain stable.

The surgeon liberally takes a pericardial patch, visualizing the two phrenic nerves while moving towards the aorta. The left phrenic nerve and left superior vena cava (SVC) are visible as the surgeon uses electrocautery to burn up to the arch of the aorta, reaching the pericardial reflection.

The surgeon lifts the thymus to locate the pericardial reflection, removing small attachments. The thymus is positioned on the aorta rather than the innominate vein. The surgeon uses precise wrist movements to maneuver the assisting hand, revealing the interdigitated fronds of the thymus between the head and neck vessels.

The surgeon uses a sub-xiphoid port to gain access after removing the thymus from the diaphragm, providing a clear view of the head and neck vessels. The carotid artery is positioned higher, while the subclavian artery is lower. The port facilitates tumor removal without rib opening and allows camera insertion for viewing the opposite side.

The surgeon forcefully tugs on the thyro-thymic ligament to divide it above the superior horn, revealing the carotid artery below the ligature. The left subclavian artery is visible at the bottom of the frame, while the trachea is located between the carotid and innominate arteries.

The surgeon clears fatty tissue interdigitated between the vessels around the arch of the aorta, safely dissecting the pericardial reflection without risk to nearby structures. The right phrenic nerve is noted to be far from the dissection site.

The surgeon divides the pericardium on the right side, aiming towards the right phrenic nerve. A sub-xiphoid camera is used to obtain a clear view of the right phrenic nerve without making right-sided incisions. The lateral mammary artery is visible on the right side, and a large disc is positioned under the thymoma.

The surgeon uses a ligature to perform intrapericardial dissection, carefully approaching the right phrenic nerve. The bipolar device is employed to dissect along the pericardium, ensuring the phrenic nerve is visible and preserved throughout the procedure.

Using a sub-xiphoid ten-millimeter camera, the surgeon achieves a clear view of the surgical field. Instruments, including a wristed Johan, are maneuvered to pull structures into the chest. The hook diathermy is used to carefully lift and avoid injury to the superior vena cava (SVC), following its path to locate the innominate vein.

The surgeon identifies a large, cord-like vascular structure near the thymoma, which appears to be a significant vein. Using a hook diathermy, the surgeon dissects the vein and applies a ligature to secure it, potentially identifying the thymic vein.

The surgeon examines the arch of the aorta, identifying the left subclavian artery and carotid artery. The dissection proceeds across the mediastinum to the right superior horn, with the reamer vein visible coming off the SVC. A small vein, possibly a vestigial innominate vein, is divided using a ligature, allowing the right horn to be pulled medially.

The surgeon uses a sub-xiphoid camera to visualize the innominate artery beneath the ligature, ensuring the safety of the SVC and innominate artery. The right superior horn is visible, and the surgeon follows the innominate artery up into the neck, pulling with the assisting arm. The view includes the left carotid artery and interdigitating tissue between the carotid and innominate, with the trachea located between them. The surgeon lifts the tissue to dissect down onto the trachea, aiming to remove all fat from it and properly extract the right horn.

The surgeon performs dissection in the superior horn, dividing small vessels within the thyrothymic ligament using energy devices. The trachea is visible in the distance as the ligament is divided. The right lung is inflated but does not obstruct the view. The arch of the aorta, innominate, carotid, and subclavian arteries are exposed with minimal surrounding fat. A large thymic sample is removed through the sub-xiphoid port, and a twenty French drain is placed through the sub-xiphoid hole.

The surgeon removes the thymic tissue, revealing a clear view of the heart with the pericardium visible as a large disc. The left atrial appendage is seen moving, and the innominate artery, left carotid, and subclavian arteries are identifiable. The trachea is exposed with minimal surrounding fat, visible through the right chest view.
